1: "You shall not sacrifice to the LORD your God an ox or a sheep that has a defect, any serious flaw whatever — that is detestable to the LORD your God" — the unblemished-sacrifice requirement: mum (H3971, defect/blemish) is a technical cultic term covering any physical imperfection. ANE sacrificial texts from Mesopotamia (ritual texts from Ur III and Old Babylonian periods) and Egypt similarly required unblemished sacrificial animals — the principle that the god deserves the best is universal. What is distinctive in Israel is the reason: YHWH is not manipulated by sacrifice quality (as if a perfect lamb secured his favor more reliably than a blemished one) but the quality requirement expresses the worshiper's acknowledgment of YHWH's worth. Malachi 1:8 makes the honor-logic explicit: would you offer that to your governor?
2: "If there is found among you, in any of your towns that the LORD your God is giving you, a man or a woman who does what is evil in the sight of the LORD your God, transgressing his covenant" — the apostate-investigation law (vv2-7): the gravity of apostasy (la'avor et-berito, transgressing his covenant) is the same as high treason in a suzerainty treaty context — the vassal who defects to another lord has committed the capital offense against the suzerain. The judicial investigation process that follows (v4: diligent inquiry, confirmed truth) prevents mob-action based on rumor and requires due process before any punishment.
3: "And has gone and served other gods and bowed down to them, or to the sun or the moon or any of the heavenly host, which I have not commanded" — the specific apostasy named: sun/moon/star worship (avoda zara) was widespread in the ANE. Mesopotamian astral religion had a developed theology of celestial bodies as deities (Shamash = sun, Sin = moon, Ishtar = Venus). Egyptian solar religion centered on Ra/Amon-Ra. The host of heaven (tseva hashamayim) was a constant temptation for Israel — 2 Kings 21:3-5 records Manasseh's construction of altars to the host of heaven in both the temple courts. The Babylonian exile was in part the consequence of exactly this apostasy (2 Kings 23:4-12 records Josiah's purge of astral cult items).
4: "And you are informed and hear about it, then you shall investigate diligently; and if it is true and confirmed that such a detestable thing has been done in Israel" — the four-stage judicial process: (1) a report (leacha hachad vehogedu lach), (2) you hear about it, (3) diligent investigation (vedarashta heitev), (4) confirmed truth (vehinei emet nachon haddavar). This is the Deuteronomic legal standard: no one is punished on report alone; the case requires thorough investigation and confirmed evidence. The "diligent investigation" standard is the basis for later rabbinic rules of cross-examination and the requirement that witnesses be rigorously tested before their testimony is accepted.
5: "Then bring that man or woman to your town gate and stone them to death" — the town-gate execution: gates (sha'ar) were the civic and legal center of ancient Israelite towns — where judges sat (Deuteronomy 16:18; Ruth 4:1-2; Amos 5:12), contracts were witnessed (Ruth 4:11), and public legal acts were performed. Execution at the gate makes the punishment a public covenant act witnessed by the entire community. The public nature was deliberate: Deuteronomy 17:13 states the purpose — "all the people shall hear and be afraid." The deterrent function requires publicity.
6: "A person shall be put to death only on the testimony of two or three witnesses; no one is to be executed on the testimony of a single witness" — the two-or-three witnesses rule: this standard (also in Numbers 35:30; Deuteronomy 19:15) becomes a constitutional principle of Jewish legal procedure. The Sanhedrin's rules of capital-case procedure as codified in Tractate Sanhedrin require multiple witnesses and extensive cross-examination. Jesus cites this principle for church discipline (Matthew 18:16) and Paul applies it to accusations against elders (1 Timothy 5:19). The two-or-three witnesses principle is the OT's most directly cited procedural rule in the NT.
7: "The witnesses shall be first to put him to death, and then all the people shall follow. So you shall purge the evil from your midst" — the witness-first requirement: the eyewitnesses who testified must be the first to throw stones. This requirement does two things: (1) it creates a significant barrier to false testimony — perjurers face executing an innocent person with their own hands; (2) it makes the entire community complicit in the execution, distributing moral responsibility for the covenant's maintenance. The community is not an audience for the court's action but a participant. The purge-formula (uvearta hara miqirbecha) is Deuteronomy's characteristic refrain after capital offenses (Deuteronomy 13:5; Deuteronomy 17:7,12; Deuteronomy 19:19; Deuteronomy 21:21; Deuteronomy 22:21-24; Deuteronomy 24:7) — the community's covenant integrity requires removing what corrupts it.
8: "If a case arises that is too difficult for your local court — involving disputed homicide, disputed legal rights, or disputed assault — cases of controversy within your towns — then you shall arise and go up to the place that the LORD your God will choose" — the supreme court provision: three categories of difficult cases — dam ledam (blood to blood, homicide cases), din ledin (legal right to legal right, civil disputes), nega laneqa (affliction to affliction, assault cases). When local courts cannot resolve these, the case goes to the central court at the sanctuary. The term "arise and go up" reflects the topography of Jerusalem — one literally went up to the hill-city. This institutional design prevents local-court impasse from generating vendetta and blood-feud.
9: "You shall go to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is serving at that time, and you shall consult them, and they shall give you the verdict" — the central court composition: Levitical priests and a lay judge together. The dual composition (priestly-legal and civic-legal) mirrors the dual authority structure of Deuteronomy throughout — priests handle cultic-religious matters, judges handle civic legal matters. For difficult cases that may combine both dimensions, the joint court is needed. Second Chronicles Deuteronomy 19:8-11 describes Jehoshaphat's court reform along these lines: priests and levites for YHWH's matters, senior men for the king's matters. This institution is the ancestor of the Sanhedrin's joint priestly-lay composition.
10: "You shall carry out the verdict they announce to you from that place the LORD will choose, and be careful to do all they instruct you" — the binding verdict requirement: the central court's decision is final and must be implemented. The court's authority derives from its location ("that place the LORD will choose") — it is YHWH's own authorized court at YHWH's chosen sanctuary. To reject its ruling is to reject YHWH's judicial authority, which is why (v12) the defiant litigant faces the death penalty.
11: "According to the instruction they give you and the verdict they pronounce, you shall act. Do not deviate from what they declare to you, to the right or to the left" — the right-left non-deviation formula here applied to the court's ruling: the same obedience that is required to YHWH's commandments (Deuteronomy 5:32; Deuteronomy 17:20; Deuteronomy 28:14) is required to the central court's verdicts. The court's authority is derivative: it speaks in YHWH's name at YHWH's place, making its rulings functionally equivalent to Torah for the purposes of covenant-community governance.
12: "The man who acts arrogantly by refusing to obey the priest who stands to minister before the LORD your God, or the judge — that man shall die. So you shall purge the evil from Israel" — the capital offense of contempt of court: the willful (zadon, presumption/arrogance) refusal to comply with the central court's ruling is a capital offense because it undermines the covenant community's governance structure. A legal system where verdicts can be ignored with impunity has no authority — the entire judicial institution depends on the enforceability of its rulings. The death penalty for contempt preserves the court's authority at the highest level. The purge-formula closes this section as it did the apostasy section (v7).
13: "And all the people shall hear and be afraid, and they will not act presumptuously again" — the deterrent purpose stated: the public consequence of defying the court (all the people shall hear) produces the fear that prevents repetition (will not act presumptuously again). The deterrent theology of Deuteronomy is systematic: public punishments have a communicative function — they speak to the community about the seriousness of the covenant's claims. The legal system is not only about retributive justice but about the community's ongoing formation in covenant-respect.
14: "When you come to the land the LORD your God is giving you and have taken possession and settled in it, and you say, 'I want to set a king over me, like all the nations that are around me'" — the anticipation of the monarchical request: Moses speaks to a future generation's demand for a king "like all the nations" (kechol hagoyim asher sevivotai). The phrase "like all the nations" is the key problem — Israel's distinctiveness as YHWH's covenant people is compromised by the desire to be like surrounding kingdoms. When Israel eventually makes this demand (1 Samuel 8:5,20), Samuel's response is framed by this Deuteronomy passage: the demand is a rejection of YHWH's kingship (1 Samuel 8:7).
15: "You may set a king over you — but he must be the one the LORD your God chooses. Set one from among your brothers as king over you; you may not put a foreigner over you, who is not your brother" — the qualified permission: Israel may have a king, but only on YHWH's terms. The divine-choice of king (asher yivchar YHWH eloheicha bo) anticipates the later election of Saul and David through prophetic designation. The native-king requirement prevents a foreign monarch from ruling Israel — the same kind of foreignness-barrier appears in all ANE royal succession law, but here it has a covenant rationale: the king must be a covenant-party brother, not an outsider with different loyalties.
16: "But he must not acquire many horses for himself or send people back to Egypt to get more horses, since the LORD has told you, 'You shall never return that way again'" — the first anti-kingship restriction: no horse-multiplication. Horses were high-value military assets in the ANE — chariotry was the ancient equivalent of air power, and possession of large horse-chariot forces marked the great powers. Egypt was the primary horse-breeder and exporter of the ancient Near East (horses were not native to Egypt but were bred in the Delta region from Hyksos-era imports). Solomon violated this restriction explicitly: 1 Kings 4:26 (40,000 stalls for horses) and Deuteronomy 10:28-29 (horses imported from Egypt and Kue). The return-to-Egypt prohibition echoes the Exodus's directional meaning: forward, not back.
17: "He must not take many wives for himself, so his heart is not led astray, nor shall he accumulate excessive silver and gold for himself" — the second and third anti-kingship restrictions: no wife-multiplication (with the explicit reason — heart-turning to other gods) and no wealth-accumulation. Solomon violated both: 1 Kings 11:3 (700 wives, 300 concubines, "his wives turned away his heart after other gods") and Deuteronomy 10:14-25 (666 talents of gold per year, silver as common as stone). The law of the king in Deuteronomy 17 reads like a pre-written indictment of the Solomonic monarchy. The three prohibitions (horses, wives, wealth) are the three pillars of ANE royal power — military strength, political alliance-marriages, and economic dominance. Deuteronomy's king law forbids all three as the basis of kingship.
18: "When he takes the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this law, verified by the Levitical priests" — the law of the royal Torah-copy: the king must personally write his own copy of the Torah, verified by the priestly custodians of the official text. This is the most remarkable royal obligation in the ancient world — no other ANE text requires the king to be a student of divine law. The Egyptian pharaoh was divine and above law; the Mesopotamian king received law from the gods as a gift to give to the people. Deuteronomy's king is a subject of the law, required to know it personally. The word "copy" (mishneh, H4932) is the origin of the book's Greek name: deuteronomion, the LXX's translation of "second law" or "copy of the law" — from which "Deuteronomy" derives.
19: "It shall be with him, and he shall read in it all the days of his life, that he may learn to fear the LORD his God by keeping all the words of this law and these statutes, and doing them" — the permanent royal reading program: the Torah-copy accompanies the king throughout his reign, and he reads it continuously (kol yemei chayav, all the days of his life). The purpose is explicitly pedagogical: to learn to fear YHWH (lemaan yilmad liyira) and to keep all the law's words. The king is not the law-giver (as in Mesopotamian royal ideology) but the law's primary student and exemplar. Josiah's response to discovering the Torah scroll (2 Kings 22:11-13 — he tore his clothes) and his subsequent reform represent this model: a king formed by Torah hearing and reading.
20: "So that his heart will not be lifted up above his brothers, and so that he will not turn from the commandment to the right or to the left, and so that he may continue long in his kingdom, he and his children, in Israel" — the anti-pride purpose: the Torah-reading is designed to prevent the king's heart from being lifted up (lom levavo al echav) above his fellow Israelites. The king is first a brother-Israelite under the same covenant before he is a ruler. The anti-pride language directly addresses the greatest danger of absolute power: the ruler who forgets his creaturely accountability before YHWH and his covenant-level equality with his subjects. The same right-left formula applied to obedience (Deuteronomy 5:32; Deuteronomy 17:11) applies here to the king's personal covenant-walking. The dynasty-promise (long reign for him and his children) is conditioned on covenant faithfulness — the same condition applied to every Israelite throughout Deuteronomy.
